skill.name: improve-codebase-architecture
skill.description: Reviews a codebase for architectural friction, shallow modules, weak seams, and refactoring opportunities using project domain language and ADR context.
skill.summary: Finds practical architecture deepening opportunities that improve testability, locality, and AI navigation.
skill.author: mattpocock

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but the reviewed files are Markdown instructions and most detections are false positives from prose, filenames, inline code formatting, and relative documentation links. No malicious command execution, credential access, network exfiltration, or prompt injection attempt was found. The real risk is operational: the skill directs broad repository exploration and may update project documentation, so users should review proposed file changes.

medium_findings.title: Broad Repository Exploration and Documentation Writes
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to read project documentation, walk the codebase with an Explore subagent, and update CONTEXT.md or offer ADR creation during the workflow. This is legitimate for architecture review, but it creates filesystem exposure and write-risk if users do not review changes.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly direct repository reading and conditional documentation edits. The behavior is core to the skill and not malicious, so the risk is operational rather than abusive.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detections
low_findings.description: The reported weak-crypto hits occur in Markdown architecture prose and headings. I found no cryptographic implementation, hash selection, encryption routine, or security-sensitive algorithm use in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of each cited line shows architecture vocabulary, headings, or Markdown references only. There is no executable code or cryptographic API use.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Ruby or Shell Backtick Execution
low_findings.description: The reported backtick detections are Markdown inline code spans for filenames, directories, and terminology. They are not Ruby backtick expressions or shell command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files are Markdown instructions, and the cited lines do not contain executable Ruby or shell syntax. The backticks mark inline text such as filenames and paths.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Path Traversal Sequence in Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The ../ sequences appear in Markdown links to sibling documentation under domain-model. They are not used to construct file paths from user input or access arbitrary filesystem locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is limited to static Markdown reference links. There is no path concatenation, file-reading code, or user-controlled traversal vector.

low_findings.title: Low-Risk Repository Reconnaissance Pattern
low_findings.description: The instruction to use an Explore subagent to walk the codebase is expected for architecture review. It can expose repository contents to the agent, but there is no evidence of exfiltration or secret harvesting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: One cited line directly requests broad exploration, while the ADR line is related workflow context. The behavior is legitimate but still deserves user awareness.

content.value_statement: Architecture reviews often miss shallow modules, scattered decisions, and weak test surfaces. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through a structured review that finds deepening opportunities grounded in project language.
actual_capabilities: Reads CONTEXT.md and ADR files before recommending architecture changes.
Identifies shallow modules, weak seams, coupling, and poor locality.
Presents numbered deepening opportunities with files, problems, solutions, and benefits.
Uses a defined architecture vocabulary for modules, interfaces, seams, adapters, depth, leverage, and locality.
Guides follow-up discussion before proposing concrete interfaces.
Can compare alternative interface designs after a candidate is selected.
limitations: It depends on the agent having enough repository access to inspect relevant files.
It does not automatically prove that a refactor is worth the delivery cost.
It may need human judgment when recommendations conflict with existing ADRs.
It focuses on architecture and testability, not performance profiling or security auditing.

use_cases.title: Refactor a tangled feature area
use_cases.description: Find modules where complexity is spread across callers and propose a deeper module shape.

use_cases.title: Prepare a codebase for AI-assisted maintenance
use_cases.description: Improve naming, locality, and test surfaces so future AI agents can navigate the code more reliably.

use_cases.title: Review architecture before a major change
use_cases.description: Use domain context and ADRs to identify which seams should change before new work begins.

prompt_templates.title: Find architecture friction
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a codebase or feature area.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the improve-codebase-architecture skill to review this repository. Start with CONTEXT.md and ADRs, then list the strongest deepening opportunities.

prompt_templates.title: Review one feature area
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused review for a known problem area.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the improve-codebase-architecture skill on the checkout flow. Identify shallow modules, weak seams, and tests that would improve after refactoring.

prompt_templates.title: Compare refactor candidates
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the skill to compare deepening opportunities in this module cluster. Rank them by locality, leverage, implementation risk, and test impact.

prompt_templates.title: Design alternative interfaces
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced follow-up after choosing a candidate.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the interface design workflow for the selected candidate. Generate several different interface options, compare their trade-offs, and recommend one.

output_examples.input: Review the billing area for architecture problems.
output: A numbered list of deepening opportunities in billing modules.
For each candidate, a problem statement, proposed direction, affected files, and testability benefits.
A prompt asking which candidate should be explored next.

output_examples.input: We selected the order intake candidate. Help shape the interface.
output: A short framing of constraints and dependencies.
Several alternative interface designs with different trade-offs.
A recommendation based on depth, locality, seam placement, and test surface.

best_practices: Run it on a focused feature area when the repository is large.
Keep CONTEXT.md and ADRs current before asking for recommendations.
Review any proposed documentation edits before accepting them.
anti_patterns: Do not use it as a substitute for product or domain decisions.
Do not accept a new seam only because it makes mocking easier.
Do not ask for concrete interfaces before choosing a refactor candidate.

faq.question: What kind of codebase is this skill for?
faq.answer: It is best for repositories where architecture, testing, or module boundaries are becoming hard to reason about.

faq.question: Does it change code automatically?
faq.answer: The skill focuses on analysis and discussion. It may update CONTEXT.md or offer ADR creation when the workflow calls for it.

faq.question: Does it require CONTEXT.md?
faq.answer: No. The skill reads CONTEXT.md when present and proceeds silently when it is missing.

faq.question: How does it use ADRs?
faq.answer: It reads ADRs to avoid reopening settled decisions unless current friction makes a decision worth revisiting.

faq.question: Can it design new interfaces?
faq.answer: Yes, but only after a candidate is selected. It then compares several interface options and recommends one.

faq.question: Is it useful with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows that can inspect repository files.
